data.title: NovaBay: 'Hold' Rating As New M&A Direction Could Evolve But With Risks
data.description:
I'm initiating coverage on NovaBay Pharmaceuticals (NBY) with a “Hold” rating. The reason why is because the company made a series of moves in 2025, selling off major assets from its pipeline. In the most recent quarterly report, it noted that it had sold off its assets of DERMAdoctor, Avenova, and PhaseOne. The initial plan was to dissolve the whole company and move on. If it sold off its assets, then why is there value left in this company? There is some value due to a change in strategic direction that has happened. The prior Chief Executive Officer (CEO), David Lazar, was able to announce a $6 million financing deal to keep the company going in August of 2025. Even though this was the CEO announced at that time, later in October of 2025 there was a leadership change. Michael Kazley was appointed as the new CEO and Chairman after several board resignations. Another positive is that the company was able to keep its stock listed on the New York Stock Exchange (NYSE). Thus, part of the value is that there is speculation that the company could acquire a new asset or end up with some type of strategic merger.

The stock gained 103% this past Friday as high volume and speculation ramped up. The premise is also that the float is low. Despite there being a total of roughly 126 million shares outstanding in stock, the float stands at just 12.39 million. The truth is that the vote to dissolve the company was there, but the CEO had a different idea in mind. There is “risk” here, and that's why I can't recommend it as a buy. A “Hold” rating is more in order because if something does materialize, then shareholders would be rewarded. On the flip side, there is never a guarantee that a deal or merger will take place. Thus, in the end it is very possible that this company could just dissolve itself in the end anyways. With the selling of its core skin care and other assets like DERMAdoctor, Avenova, and PhaseOne, plus the potential to acquire a product/merge with another company, I believe that NovaBay Pharmaceuticals should be initiated with a “Hold” rating.

Several Moves Line Up Possible Strategic Change For New Chapter

As I noted above, NovaBay Pharmaceuticals throughout 2025 had divested a lot of its assets. This means it got rid of its original business and is now evaluating several strategic alternatives. What I'm going to go over in this section are several items that likely collectively caused the stock price to trade higher by 103% this past Friday to around $19.16 per share. With the biggest one being the momentum of a possible transaction to bring about a major change to the company.

The first reason or move that may have led to the eventual rise in stock price would have been the divestiture of the company's assets. The company moved quickly in 2025 to sell off its products as part of its pipeline. The three products in question are as follows:

  • DERMAdoctor
  • Avenova
  • PhaseOne

The sale of Avenova as part of its eye unit was closed on January 17, 2025. This product contained pure hypochlorous acid (HOCl) and was responsible for treating patients' eyes. Specifically, it was used as an eyelid and eyelash cleaner for certain disorders such as the following:

  • Dry eye disease (DED)
  • Blepharitis
  • Other lid disorders where bacteria/debris played a role

With the deal itself being closed in the early part of 2025, this was the third and final deal that had gone through. The second deal happened around the same time frame, where on January 8, 2025, it sold off its PhaseOne unit. As far as the first divestiture of assets, this dealt with the selling off of its primary skin business, DERMAdoctor, on March 12, 2024. The point here is that this is the first item to show that this company went in a different strategic direction.

The second move that may have led to the rise in stock price would be the board of the company itself implementing a new direction. Why do I believe this to be the case? Quite simply, the vote to move forward with dissolution was there. If the Board deemed it appropriate to do so, it could have just dissolved the remaining company. However, it was decided against making such a move by the Board, and thus, setting up what ultimately led to the recent surge in stock price. The Plan of Dissolution vote happened at the 2025 special meeting on April 16, 2025, and was approved after. That's why the Board could have just agreed to move forward dissolving the company as opposed to continuing on as a business entity.

The third reason is because of the prior CEO being implemented for NovaBay Pharmaceuticals. In addition to the cash flow that came along with him at that period. What happened is that David Lazar became the new CEO of this company in August of 2025. Besides being a new leader for the company, he had made a $6 million investment. However, as I noted above, there was a new CEO appointed shortly thereafter in October of 2025 by the name of Michael Kazley. The prior CEO, though, drove much of the speculative merger and/or acquisition that the company was to focus on. This was the stated move for investment and the new role in the press release by the prior CEO, David Lazar, as follows:

“In making this significant investment in NovaBay, I look forward to maintaining NovaBay’s public listing and actively exploring strategic opportunities to drive value for our stockholders”

The hope is that it can somehow enact a strategic alternative that might be beneficial to NovaBay shareholders. For example, it could end up being an acquisition to bring forth a new product/pipeline, a reverse merger, or the selling of the company itself. On the flip side, what is important to note is that there is no guarantee of such a strategic alternative happening. Especially if the new CEO, Michael Kazley, and the company end up taking a different direction. Although the stock price has traded higher on speculation that something could possibly be announced soon.

The fourth reason for the high interest in the company's stock would be on the basis of a positive listing outcome by the NYSE and also additional funding. The original plan was dissolution for sure, as I have laid out above. However, if the company were to dissolve, I don't believe it would look to stay afloat on the NYSE for an extended period of time. Well, that's exactly what transpired relating to its listing on this exchange. The problem stemmed from April 18, 2024, when it was notified by this exchange that it was not in compliance with the minimum stockholders' equity. However, things have gone well since then, as the company was able to regain its footing with respect to this issue. With that being said, on October 20, 2025, it was notified by the NYSE that it did meet the requirements for continued listing. This is important because it is just one of many items I have gone over above that puts the possibility of dissolution out of the realm for now. That's not to say that things can't go south later on, because they still can.

Financials

Another point to make with NovaBay Pharmaceuticals and why it is in a different position is because of its financials. With the $393,000 it had in cash, plus the net proceeds from the issuance of Series E Preferred Stock and October 2025 Pre-Funded Warrants, it believes that it has enough cash on hand to fund its operations through November 7, 2026. This is less than a 1-year's worth of cash runway, and as such, I believe that it is going to dilute to raise cash very soon. The first reason is because its stock price traded higher by 103% to $19.16 per share on increased volume and speculation. Thus, management is going to take advantage of this and likely raise funds. Plus, there is already a financial tool in place that NovaBay could choose to utilize if it deems it necessary to do so.

This would be with respect to the S-3/A registration of securities that took place on November 25, 2025, and then was declared effective by the SEC the very next day on November 26, 2025. The proposal for this is that NovaBay could sell up to $200 million worth of units (mixed as stock, preferred stock, debt securities, warrants, and units) in order to raise funds. It doesn't have to use this, but my guess is that it might, considering the run-up it had in its share price over the last few weeks (especially with Friday's gain of 103%). The thing is that this company, even with some product revenue, is operating at a loss each quarter. In the most recent quarter, it showed that for the 3 months ending September 30, 2025, it lost $1.25 million (operating loss 10-Q SEC Filing, page #35).

Risks To Business

There are several risks that investors should be aware of before investing in NovaBay Pharmaceuticals. The first risk to consider would be the huge jump in share price of the company. High volume came in, and it was on the basis of speculation that it might be nearing a merger and/or acquisition transaction. The risk here is that there is no guarantee that such a transaction is going to take place. Even if a merger or strategic alternative is achieved, there is no assurance that it will be on acceptable terms and that shareholders will end up being happy with it.

The second risk goes to the notion of its cash on hand. As I stated above, it received a $6 million investment from the prior CEO, David Lazar, so that it didn't have to dissolve. Along with the other financial transactions that the company had done afterwards to raise cash, like issuing Series E Preferred Stock and the October 2025 Pre-Funded Warrants that it had issued. It believes that it has enough cash on hand to fund its operations through November 7, 2026. The risk here is that there is no assurance that it will be able to continue to receive additional cash from other sources. Especially since it really doesn't have a pipeline anymore. In addition, there is risk of dilution for shareholders as well. That is, management might choose to raise funds in order to continue to fund itself as a business entity.

Both of the risks above are ones worth considering, but there is a third and final risk here that might be a possibility. That is, the risk of bankruptcy. The intention is to forgo the dissolution and enact some type of strategic alternative. There is no guarantee that it will be able to find a company wanting to merge or perform a reverse merger for starters. Nor that it will be able to acquire a new product or pipeline to keep itself going as a fully-fledged biotech company. If management fails to secure some type of transaction, then there is a chance the company could just end up being dissolved. If this were to happen, then shareholders would get back much less than they invested.

Conclusion

NovaBay Pharmaceuticals was able to divest several of its assets, as I have stated above. These include DERMAdoctor, Avenova, and PhaseOne. The thing is that this company is just trading around speculation at the moment. Plus, the high volume was seen in this stock this past Friday (January 9, 2025). Its average trading volume is only 1.2 million shares, but several days ago, in one day, it reached 10 million shares. Today, though (January 12, 2025), the stock is trading lower by 33% on 2.37 million volume. This brings up the risk noted above in that the stock climbed on speculation of a merger/acquisition. Thus, at the same rate the stock traded higher on the back of this, it could immediately reverse lower. With a change in direction starting off with the prior CEO David Lazard (plus a $6 million investment from him), along with the ability to possibly implement a strategic alternative that might happen under new CEO Michael Kazley, I believe that NovaBay's stock should be initiated with a “Hold” rating. I can't condone a “Buy” or “Strong Buy” rating because at the present time there is nothing super concrete about its long-term prospects.

data.title: U.S. December CPI: Core Goods Weakness Continued To Undermine Tariff Concerns
data.description:
The December CPI report closes out the year with a soft view of inflationary pressures. The headline figure was shaped by the volatile components, where a sharp move in food prices was largely offset by a more muted energy print, keeping overall inflation from shifting much. But the more important signal this month came from the core details, where the downside surprise was driven by softer goods inflation even as services, particularly shelter, remained firm. Taken together, the report reinforces that inflation pressures continue to cool in aggregate, while also offering an important insight on a key policy question: whether tariffs are translating into sustained consumer price acceleration, or only creating temporary noise in a handful of import-sensitive categories.

CPI

U.S. CPI increased 0.3% MoM and 2.7% YoY in December, meeting analyst expectations that the headline inflation rate would be basically unchanged. The annual rate of 2.7% is unchanged from the contentious November print and consistent with readings in June and July of last year. There were competing forces within the volatile components that offset each other, keeping the headline inflation rate from moving much.

  • The food component increased 0.7% MoM in December, the largest monthly increase since October 2022, driven by the food at home index increasing at the same high rate of 0.7% MoM, the highest monthly increase since August 2022. There was breadth in the gains in grocery prices, with 5/6 of the major indexes rising. The smallest subsegment, other food at home, saw the largest increase of 1.6% MoM as sugar & sweets (+0.8% MoM), fats & oils (+1.8% MoM), and spices, condiments & sauces (+1.9% MoM) printed hot. The smaller food-away-from-home index was also up 0.7% MoM, the largest since October 2022.
  • Offsetting the strong gains in food prices was a cooler energy segment increase of 0.3% MoM in December. Within the index, a large 4.4% MoM increase in the natural gas index was offset by gas prices down -0.5% MoM (NSA gas price index down -5.3% MoM) and the electricity index down -0.1% MoM. The monthly rise in utility natural gas services was the largest since January 2023.

The standout among the volatile components was a surprisingly strong monthly increase in the food segment. This likely reflects an underestimation of the November Food index rather than a meaningful shift in underlying inflation dynamics. In last month’s partial report, the BLS showed food prices rising just 0.06% from September to November, which effectively implied near-zero monthly gains in both October and November. Against that backdrop, the hot December increase looks more like a statistical payback from an unusually soft November reading than evidence of a broader reacceleration in food inflation.

Core CPI

U.S. core CPI inflation, which excludes food and energy, increased 0.2% MoM and 2.6% YoY in December, missing expectations of a 0.3% MoM monthly print and the annual rate accelerating to 2.7% YoY. The downside surprise was mostly a result of goods segments coming in weaker than expected, while services segments posted another month of moderate gains. This continues a string of prints that point to cooling core price pressures and an annual rate that is the lowest since March 2021.

Here are some relevant details within core CPI:

  • The core goods segment saw no change on a monthly basis in December, the weakest since May 2025. However, there was variability in the monthly rates of the indexes within the core goods segment. Household goods (+0.5% MoM) and apparel (+0.6% MoM) both posted strong increases in December, and medical care goods (+0.3% MoM) saw a moderate rise. These were offset by declines in transportation goods (-0.3% MoM), education & communication goods (-2.0% MoM), and alcoholic beverages (-0.1% MoM).
  • Two key indexes that drove the softness in core goods were the new vehicles and the used cars & trucks indexes, printing 0.0% MoM and -1.1% MoM, respectively. The decline in the latter index was the largest since July 2024. Weakness in vehicle prices has been a factor in weakening core inflation, likely driven by consumers’ demand for autos softening in the face of a weakening labor market.
  • The core services segment increased 0.3% MoM in December, the hottest since August. The shelter index was the main reason for this, rising 0.4% MoM (also the hottest since August), but not necessarily all due to rent and home prices. Lodging away from home jumped 2.9% MoM, the strongest since September 2023. Outside of shelter, there were moderate to strong increases in medical care services (+0.4% MoM), transportation services (+0.5% MoM), and recreation services (+1.8% MoM; largest ever)

Going through the details of the core components, it’s clear that there was a lot of variability in both services and goods prices in December. The larger core subsegments, new & used motor vehicles (-0.4% MoM) and shelter (+0.4% MoM) came close to offsetting each other, which suggests that other indexes were in aggregate pointing to a slight weakening in core price pressures. Indeed, the BLS prints a CPI excluding food, energy, used vehicles, and shelter index, and it was up just 0.2% MoM and 2.3% YoY, just slightly above the Fed’s 2% target.

In the month’s search for evidence that tariffs are causing a significant uptick, we find very little. Yes, household goods and apparel both did see moderately strong increases in December (both indexes that should be sensitive to import duties on consumer goods), but the broader short-term trend in core goods inflation is turning over. Specifically, the 3-month annualized rate is down to 1.1%, the lowest since June. This looks more like evidence that tariffs caused a temporary increase in inflation, consistent with the Fed’s base case.

Market Reaction

Markets have been slightly volatile in trading the CPI data, but the general bias has been dovish. That bias has been driven by the slight downside surprise of core CPI inflation, overshadowing the hot prints in food and shelter.

  • S&P futures (SPX) jumped right after the CPI data was released, but the initial rise was sold off to a more moderate gain. In the end, the S&P 500 (SP500) opened roughly unchanged and has moved lower by about -2% in the first half hour of trading. NASDAQ (COMP:IND) is faring slightly better at around 0.0%, and the small-cap index, the Russell 2000 (RTY), is very slightly higher by 0.1%. The move in equities doesn’t seem to have been impacted by CPI as much as the moves in bond yields.
  • Treasury yields dipped immediately after the release of the CPI, but the move lower was reversed going into the open of the stock market. Yields maintained a slight decline around 10 am; however, with long-end yields like the 10-year seeing larger declines. The differences in the moves along the yield curve likely reflect the bond market attributing slightly weaker inflationary pressure to weaker economic fundamentals.
  • The U.S. dollar has had a similar reaction to short-term bond yields, falling sharply at first before recovering later in the morning. The U.S. dollar index (DXY) is actually slightly positive as of 10 am as it strengthens against several major currencies.

While the CPI report can mostly be assessed as weak, the implications for the Fed are not that important. The benign print still has a bit of uncertainty built into it via the messy November report, and the decision to pause in January (January 28-29th meeting) has looked more or less locked in regardless of this data (FedWatch January pause probability > 95%). Perhaps the most important thing that FOMC members can take away from this report is that the end of year data can be used as further evidence that tariffs are not causing a sustained increase in inflation. But this appears to have been the Fed’s base case since the summer, allowing it to be less sensitive to the upside risks in the inflation outlook.

I look forward to feeling a bit more confident in inflation data, given that the Q4 CPI reports have shown that the government shutdown disruption produced a significant impairment to the BLS’s ability to provide reliable statistics. The broader market and Fed likely feel the same way, causing there to be a noticeable amount of ambivalence in the December CPI reaction.

data.title: Snowflake: Buy The Dip On Lower Valuation And Rising RPO (Upgrade)
data.description:
To me, 2026 is not a year for passive investing. The stock market is sitting near all-time highs, and my macro view is that the S&P 500 will end 2026 flat to slightly down. That said, I think there is still plenty of opportunity for investors to generate alpha by deploying selective stock-picking, especially in names that have seen recent volatility.

Snowflake Inc. (SNOW), in my view, is a great example of a large-cap growth stock that is taking a recent breather. The stock remains up ~35% over the past year but is down nearly 20% from recent peaks near $280 notched in October, as investors began to fret about slowing growth rates.

I last wrote a "Sell" article on Snowflake in October, when the stock was last trading near $250. Since then, Snowflake's valuation has moderated considerably, and because the stock isn't a SaaS firm, I'm more accepting of the lumpier growth rates that Snowflake exhibits. I see an opportunity for a near-term rebound here, and I'm giving the stock a double upgrade to "Buy," which isn't something I commonly do, especially with a large-cap momentum name.

These are the core highlights behind my vastly improved sentiment on Snowflake:

  • Enormous RPO growth, particularly in Q3. Snowflake generates product revenue that is more variable from quarter to quarter based on customer usage, but it does have minimum customer commitments. Recently, its backlog of commitments saw sharp growth, particularly in the long-term bucket. To me, this is a clear indication that customers are comfortable investing heavily in Snowflake architecture for the long haul.
  • Scaled customer expansion and strong retention rates in a tougher macro backdrop. A growing share of Snowflake's customers are large titans, generating over $1 million in annual product revenue. On top of this, Snowflake's net revenue retention is easily clearing above 120%. Many software companies, meanwhile, have struggled with retention rates in a tougher economy, particularly seat-based software companies that are contending with slower headcount growth or layoffs at their customers.
  • Economies of scale. Pro forma operating margins are roughly doubling, as Snowflake is capitalizing on its larger sales base. We continue to be wary of Snowflake's headcount growth as it invests in R&D, but as long as margins continue to expand, we remain comfortable on the profitability front.

From a valuation perspective, it would certainly be a stretch to call Snowflake cheap. That said, its valuation multiples are now sitting at much more reasonable levels than at any other point in recent history. At current share prices near $220, Snowflake trades at a market cap of $75.38 billion. After we net off the $4.39 billion of cash against $2.28 billion of debt on Snowflake's most recent balance sheet, the company's resulting enterprise value is $73.27 billion.

For FY27 (the year for Snowflake ending in January 2027), Wall Street analysts have pegged a consensus revenue estimate of $5.78 billion for the company, which reflects 24% y/y growth. Considering recent growth trends in the ~30% range and especially the company's buildup of RPO, we think that this estimate may be on the conservative front. Nevertheless, taking this forecast at face value, Snowflake trades at 12.7x EV/FY27 revenue.

What makes Snowflake more appealing today is the fact that while its valuation has moderated over the past quarter, many of its peers in the 20%-30% growth software bucket have seen meaningful expansion in multiples as the stock market rallied into 2025's year-end. Snowflake still sits at the higher end of its peer multiples, but given its RPO growth and potential acceleration in revenue, I find now to be an excellent entry point.

To me, it's a great time to take advantage of the dip in Snowflake to buy.

Q3 Download

Let's now go through Snowflake's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q3 earnings summary is shown below:

Snowflake's revenue surged 29% y/y to $1.21 billion, beating Wall Street's expectations of $1.18 billion (+26% y/y) by a three-point margin. While we note that revenue growth did decelerate versus 32% years ago/y growth in Q2, we also have to consider the fact that the AWS outage on October 20 within the quarter impacted end-customer consumption to the tune of about $1-$2 million in revenue loss, per management's remarks on the Q&A portion of the Q3 earnings call.

Where we are primarily focused is on Snowflake's buildup in remaining performance obligations, which is essentially customer backlog waiting to be recognized as revenue.

It's clear from Snowflake's RPO growth, shown in the chart above, that AI is a true business driver for the company, as the number of companies investing in their backend infrastructure has driven a flow-through of spending to infrastructure platforms like Snowflake. RPO grew at a rapid 37% y/y pace to $7.88 billion. More impressive yet is the sequential $949 million increase in RPO versus Q2, versus only $245 million added in Q2 versus Q1.

We note that 48% of the company's $7.88 billion in RPO is expected to convert to revenue within 12 months, or $3.78 billion. This already represents 65%, or roughly two-thirds, of Wall Street's revenue consensus for FY27. To me, this indicates a high degree of revenue visibility and a good chance for outperformance, as RPO for a company like Snowflake essentially reflects minimum commitments.

Meanwhile, the company's net revenue retention rates clocked in at 125%, consistent with the prior few quarters. Again, many software companies have struggled with retention rates recently amid the tougher macro.

Snowflake notes that ~50% of its new business is centered around customers seeking AI infrastructure, and the company's AI products have reached a $100 million annualized run rate one quarter ahead of schedule. Per CEO Sridhar Ramaswamy's remarks on the Q3 earnings call:

$100 million in AI revenue run rate achieved 1 quarter earlier than anticipated, thanks to our pace of innovation, cross-functional collaboration and early adoption among many of our marquee customers. Because we operate as a consumption-based business, this number reflects real-world enterprise usage. It's a direct signal of how customers are using our AI capabilities in production to create value today.

What's more, our AI capabilities are strengthening our customer relationships and expanding the value we deliver across every stage of the data life cycle. AI is a key driver of the strength that we see in our core business. In Q3, we landed a record number of new logos and continue to build strong momentum with AI influencing 50% of the bookings signed this quarter. We also deepened relationships with existing customers as 28% of all use cases deployed during the quarter incorporated AI.

Snowflake is also scaling with efficiency in mind. As shown below, pro forma operating margins leaped 5 points y/y (nearly double) to 11%:

Key Takeaways

To me, Snowflake's RPO growth, its ~30% revenue growth, and its lofty net retention rates are all clear signals that Snowflake is achieving true AI-driven tailwinds and is bucking a weaker macroeconomy in which many software companies have showcased meaningful deceleration in growth. Take advantage of this dip as a buying opportunity.

data.title: IREN: Cheap Valuation Means I Am Doubling Down
data.description:
IREN Limited (IREN) stock has lost approximately one-third of its value since its early November peak when the stock traded above $75. Such a significant dip occurred after the FQ1 2026 earnings release, which looks more like something sentiment-driven because earnings were strong and confirmed the bullish case to me. The recent CES 2026 event confirmed that the demand for compute capacity is still growing aggressively, which is a strong bullish catalyst for IREN. With strong AI tailwinds, IREN's revenue is poised to demonstrate aggressive growth over the long-term. Furthermore, the valuation became much more attractive after the dip. The stock is down by 17% since my previous writeup while fundamentals remain promising-looking, meaning that I am doubling down with a 'Strong buy' reiteration.

Fundamental analysis

IREN gained 261% in 2025, while the S&P 500 Index increased by approximately 17% over the same period. Such a substantial outperformance is explained by the management's stellar execution amid massive tailwinds for the entire AI infrastructure industry. We see that AI capabilities penetrating the modern world aggressively as the number of use cases is consistently expanding. The below slide from McKinsey speaks volumes about AI's potential over the long haul. We see that AI agents are used across multiple industries to automate various functions within organizations. Of course, some industries and functions show lower or even zero adoption rates.

Most of the AI adoption projects are still in pilot phases, but it is absolutely normal for such young technology. Even if AI capabilities are able to automate only back-office functions across all industries, it still will be a huge multi-trillion industry. According to the World Bank, the total labor force of the U.S. is around 175 million. Let's conservatively assume that AI will be able to automate 20% of all jobs, which is around 35 million employees nationwide. The average annual U.S. salary is approximately $67k. Multiplying 35 million employees by the average salary yields more than $2 trillion, which is close to 7% of the U.S. GDP, a vast amount. And we are speaking only about one country, the United States. I did this calculation just to show vast potential of AI capabilities to create economic value for the world. Of course, it might be a bumpy road for the technology because there will always be controversy related to social aspects because an increase in unemployment by even a few percentage points due to AI will mean millions of people losing their jobs and income. However, we should understand that AI has absolutely enormous potential to create economic value over the long term.

Given generative AI's potential to create economic value, soaring demand for compute capacity looks absolutely reasonable. During the recent CES 2026 event, Nvidia's key executives said that the demand for their AI accelerators continues growing as the AI revolution is currently shifting to the inferencing phase, which requires even more compute power than training. Therefore, IREN's strong FQ1 2026 earnings release with revenue and EPS notably surpassing consensus estimates is unlikely to be a one-off event. During the earnings call, the management said that the company has already secured several multi-year contracts, including the one with Microsoft. With several large contracts behind the back, the management's target to achieve $3.4 billion in annualized AI cloud revenue by the end of 2026 does not look unrealistic. The best part is that the management forecasts the Microsoft contract to generate an 85% project EBITDA margin, which is ultra-high profitability level.

Finally, recent movements in the RSI indicator suggests that IREN is regaining investors' confidence. We can see that the metric has stagnated between 45 and the oversold level of 30. However, it break through 45 since the start of 2026, which might indicate rapidly improving investors' sentiment around the stock.

Valuation analysis

In my previous thesis, I said that valuation uncertainty is a big fundamental risk. After a 17% dip since my previous call and the management's announcement about their target to achieve $3.4 billion in annualized sales by the end of this year, the valuation risk became much lower. The company's current market cap is circa $15 billion, and $3.4 billion in annualized sales means a forward P/S ratio of below 5, which does not look like a rich valuation for a company supported by massive secular tailwinds.

In addition, let's not forget that IREN is already EPS profitable, which means we have the forward P/E ratio forecast available. The estimated FY2028 forward P/E ratio of just 16 looks extremely attractive given the fact that IREN is at early stages of its life cycle. Such cheap valuation could have been justified if IREN was drowning in debt, but it is not the case as the company's total debt is several times lower than the market cap. Moreover, IREN ended FQ1 2026 with a net cash position.

The assertion that IREN is substantially undervalued is also supported by Wall Street's consensus target price of $83. It implies a large 65% upside potential, which is a compelling upside.

Mitigating factors

IREN currently trades at $50, which might be a strong resistance level as it represents a notable psychological milestone. I believe it might be a notable share price headwind because the stock has already tried to break through this level since the big post-earnings dip, but these attempts were not successful. If this time the stock fails to break through $50, it might disappoint investors and create a perception that IREN has already reached its maximum potential. Under this scenario, we might see another sell-off wave, and it can potentially result in another dip.

IREN's share price might suffer if there is panic in the broader market as it is a high-beta growth stock. This year started with several developments, which significantly increased the level of geopolitical and political uncertainty. And if the military operation of the U.S. in Venezuela was quite far from the stock market, the recent reports about Federal prosecutors opening criminal investigation of the Fed's Chairman, Jerome Powell, might make investors really nervous. The entire world's trust in the U.S. stock market was primarily based on political stability of the country and independence of the Fed from the U.S. Government and President. If this trust deteriorates, we might see international investors fleeing, which might trigger a panic.

Conclusion

I think that the recent dip presents a compelling buying opportunity for long-term investors as IREN looks really cheap at the moment. The deep discount does not look justified as IREN boasts a healthy balance sheet and it is well positioned to capitalize on powerful AI tailwinds.

data.title: AST SpaceMobile: Starlink Is No Match For Its Premium Space-Based Connectivity Ambitions
data.description:
Investment thesis

The company AST SpaceMobile (ASTS) is entering a new stage of development. December 24, 2025 saw the successful launch of the first BlueBird6 satellite, which, thanks to its scale and bandwidth, finally makes it possible to create a unique and reliable network of uninterrupted broadband communications. Therefore, it is my recommendation to "Buy" ASTS shares, since the company is getting closer to its main goal. Even despite fierce competition from Starlink, absence of regulatory risks, easier satellite deployment (because a larger number of satellites in near-Earth orbit creates potential risks of regulators aiming to combat space debris and orbital congestion), ASTS can be expected to occupy a premium communications niche, collaborating directly with international telecom companies and national telecom operators.

AST SpaceMobile's strategy and growth drivers

AST SpaceMobile is a unique company that is creating its own cellular broadband network using space satellites. Their first real prototype is the BlueWalker 3 satellite (launched on September 10, 2022), which was followed by the launch of the first commercial BlueBird satellites (first launch on August 21, 2024). ASTS's main feature is its space-based broadband architecture, enabling the lowest possible communication latency. These satellites form and direct wireless beams into cells to make communication seamless and reliable, at least as much as cellular communication from mobile operators' ground towers.

ASTS has entered a new phase in its satellite launch roadmap, with the successful launch of the BlueBird 6 satellite on December 24, 2025. In the company's words, it is "the largest commercial communications system ever deployed in low Earth orbit." The launch of this satellite takes communications to a new level, allowing data transfer speeds of up to 120 Mbps. BlueBird 6 is three times larger than previous satellites and provides data transfer speeds that are ten times faster in terms of bandwidth. ASTS plans to launch 45 to 60 satellites by the end of 2026.

Beyond space tech and launching its own satellites, ASTS has a competitive edge in forming new partnerships with major mobile operators. As an example, the company signed a deal with Vodafone on December 9, 2024, that will provide broadband to subscribers in Africa and Europe until 2034. The main partnership in the US is with Verizon. They formed a strategic partnership in May 2024, though the financial terms of the deal weren't announced. Certain analysts thought this partnership wouldn't lead to any specific decisions or contracts. On October 8, 2025, they signed an agreement to provide direct connectivity services to the operator's customers starting in 2026. As a presentation, there was a successful demonstration of direct voice and video calls, along with two-way messaging between an unmodified smartphone and the BlueBird satellite in space. For Verizon, the main goal of this partnership is to increase mobile coverage in rural areas and hard-to-reach places in the US.

ASTS is also working to establish partnerships in regional markets. The agreement with Saudi Arabia's operator, stc Group, will allow the company to cover not only the Arabian Peninsula market, but also other regional markets in the Middle East and North Africa. A 10-year partnership agreement has been signed, which provides for commercial services worth $175 million.

Competition between ASTS and Starlink

ASTS's main challenge is not only to launch satellites and deploy a reliable broadband network, it's also to compete with tech giant Starlink, whose active mobile service is already available on regular smartphones. If the ASTS network requires the launch of 45 to 60 satellites to be fully operational, then Starlink already has more than 650 satellites launched with SpaceX that offer Direct to Cell functionality. The first SMS transmissions were launched in 2025 in partnership with T-Mobile and other operators, while full-scale voice and data transmission is expected to be implemented in 2026. Furthermore, in my personal experience living in Ukraine, I have already encountered the successful operation of the Starlink service, which, in cooperation with the largest Ukrainian operator, Kyivstar, have made SMS notifications available in areas outside of coverage for both critical services and the population in frontline areas.

I would like to compare the key characteristics of ASTS and Starlink in the table below to determine how they differ in terms of competition. An analytical perspective reveals that Starlink has a commercial advantage because it is part of the vast SpaceX ecosystem. Furthermore, Starlink is already generating profits. But the space tech from ASTS is pretty unique. It's one of those times when quantity doesn't really matter. What's important is quality, because the ASTS satellites are significantly larger, so it could become the tech standard in 2026 and grab a premium spot in the communications market. On top of that, there could be some regulatory issues for Starlink. That's especially true in regional markets where the company's operations cause problems for ground-based towers (thanks to the Ka and Ku bands that the company uses, and which might already be taken by local operators). Starlink often operates as an independent provider, causing national regulators to take protectionist measures to protect local operators.

Criterion

ASTS

Starlink

Business Model

Partnership with the largest operators on a B2B model.

An ecosystem with SpaceX that allows direct competition.

Technology

A small number of giant satellites with enormous bandwidth.

A large number of small satellites with a mass target.

Relations with regulators

No regulatory risks and strong support from operators and traditional telecom companies.

Potential problems with national regulators, as well as difficulties in obtaining frequencies due to interference with terrestrial networks.

AST SpaceMobile's Financial Profile

It's no surprise that AST SpaceMobile doesn't have a strong financial profile, given that the company's main products and technologies are just now moving from prototypes to implementation. Also, commercialization hasn't really started yet, which means the company's profits are still negative. The latest data for Q3 2025 shows GAAP EPS of -$0.45. The key point is that this is $0.18 less than the consensus estimate of analysts. Data on revenue forecasts was also worse than expected. The figure was $14.73 million, compared to analysts' expectations of $7.31 million more. However, in spite of this, it confirmed its revenue forecast for the second half of 2025 in the range of $50 million to $75 million. Sometime around February 10, or so, we'll find out if the company managed to hit its product commercialization goals, generating record revenue for Q4 2025.

My opinion is that the chances of commercialization are really high, because the company already has contractual commitments for revenue from its partners that are over $1 billion. It shows that there's a lot of demand for ASTS as it moves toward launching its commercial services. A financial safety net is provided by cash and liquidity totaling $1.2 billion. Taking into account that the company's total operating expenses for Q3 2025 amounted to $94.4 million, if operating expenses remain at the same level, this reserve could last for a full three years (more than 12 quarters). That said, because operating expenses are going up by an average of 10-20% each quarter, it seems like a more realistic estimate of ASTS's financial strength would be two years. This estimate does not take into account the fact that the company's projected liquidity is already $3.2 billion. These include the expected $2 billion that should come in from new bond issues and credit lines.

Risks of AST SpaceMobile

Based on my recommendation to "Buy" ASTS, I see the risks of this investment idea as being directly related to the threat factors that could impact its performance. As part of the company's conference following the presentation of its quarterly report for Q3 2025, the management highlighted the risks associated with providing different options for achieving the projected financial results, given that the achievement of this goal depends on the successful launch and deployment of satellites. Moreover, senior management confirmed that there are no guarantees that the projected indicators will be achieved, since there are risks associated with delays in satellite launches, integration of the private spectrum, and the stability of the capital structure.

ASTS purchase price

My main concern is the high market volatility of the company's shares. Their price has already shown a massive increase of 336% over the past 12 months. In the opinion of many Wall Street analysts, its current price is overheated. When you look at the stock chart, it's easy to see why. All in all, high ASTS prices are the main reason for doubts about the need to buy its shares. Nevertheless, in anticipation of a technical correction in the chart, I am prepared to buy at a certain price level.

I suggest looking at the 1D time frame chart to determine the purchase price of ASTS shares. Traditional technical analysis allows us to identify a new upward trend channel. Its lower support line is currently at $57.00. Important support levels are also represented by horizontal lines at $61.40 and $69.69. Price range $61.40–$69.69 forms a key support zone, where I expect the stock to continue growing. But, of course, for this to happen, we need a technical price correction. Given that the historical high for ASTS is $102.79, is within reach. Each time the stock reached new highs in the past, its price underwent a technical correction of 10-40%. I therefore expect the price to behave in the same way this time, with the renewed historic high allowing the shares to fall below the support zone of $61.40–$69.69. Once we reach this price zone, it will be time to open new buy positions. In the meantime, however, caution is advised with new purchases until this correction occurs.

Conclusion

Overall, results from fundamental analysis of AST SpaceMobile suggest that its shares are highly attractive for investment. ASTS's continued growth is mainly driven by the successful launch of its first satellite, BlueBird 6, as well as new partnership agreements with Verizon and stc Group. Taking into account that ASTS shares are currently overbought, I recommend a "Buy" rating, meaning that new long positions should be opened after a technical correction. I think the best price range for buying is between $61.40–$69.69.

data.title: Cardiff Oncology: What Storms Can We Expect In 2026? The Case For Measured Enthusiasm
data.description:
Topline Summary and Update

Cardiff Oncology (CRDF) has been an interesting story to watch unfold. In particular, 2025 was momentous after seeing the first readout of their randomized, phase 2 trial in first-line colorectal cancer, leading to a breakout for the stock and a lot of anticipation for a more robust update. After that came in July, the market did not receive it with optimism, and I offered my thoughts in an article shortly thereafter. Since then, it's been relatively quiet, and as we head into the next round of data updates, I want to make the case for the investment thesis again, although with some significant caveats.

Pipeline Updates

Onvansertib

The song remains the same for CRDF, with the entire investment thesis resting on the success of a single molecule targeting a heretofore unexploited target: PLK1. I've offered my thoughts on this approach in past articles, including my most recent analysis focused on describing what we learned coming out of the randomized, phase 2 CRDF-004 trial.

In short, back in July we got a look at a readout that included a total of 110 patients divided across 3 treatment arms: 1 standard of care arm, and 2 arms combing 1 of 2 doses of onvansertib with chemotherapy. These were evaluated in patients with newly diagnosed, KRAS-wildtype metastatic colorectal cancer, which has seen little in the way of change to the standard of care.

We have definitely seen innovation in this space, particularly with the advent of BRAF inhibition and immune checkpoint inhibition. However, these treatments are limited to patients who have uncommon biomarkers. CRDF is hoping to make a bigger splash. And CRDF-004's update was a little disappointing in that regard, since response rates fell (from 64% to 49% in the higher-dose arm and from 50% to 42% in the lower-dose arm). Both appear to be performing better than the control treatment (response rate of 30%), but it's definitely less impressive than what we saw in the preliminary readout.

Initial looks at duration of response and progression-free survival were encouraging for onvansertib, but they did not paint a clear picture of benefit with onvansertib. The next major catalyst that we can expect for the onvansertib story is an update to this trial, which CRDF anticipates will come in the first quarter of 2026. Beyond that, there have been no major updates since my last article in terms of the gastrointestinal oncology story.

But they did make some progress in a new indication. At the ASH meeting in Orlando back in December, investigators presented the first results of a phase 1 trial evaluating the use of onvansertib in patients with chronic myelomonocytic leukemia. This analysis included 9 patients, and among those who had received at least one cycle of therapy, 38% achieved either a hematologic or bone marrow response to treatment.

This was an investigator-initiated trial, so it is not clear whether CRDF has CMML as a disease on its radar, but it does give one alternative direction for the research to head as a continuation of the colorectal and pancreatic cancer stories.

Financial Overview

As of the most recent quarterly filing, CRDF held $10.1 million in cash and equivalents, as well as $50.5 million in short-term investments. Operating expenses were $12.1 million for the quarter, and after interest and a small amount of royalty revenue, the net loss was $11.2 million.

For the first 9 months of 2025, CRDF used $32.0 million in cash to fund operations. At this burn rate, the implied cash runway for the company is approximately 8 to 9 quarters, consistent with a management-guided cash runway into Q1 2027.

Strengths and Risks

I still do think the case for CRDF remains in decent shape, although we have not heard any momentous announcements in the past few months. Here are some things that come to mind in January 2026 for this story.

Strength - CRDF has been able to deliver on important data updates over the past few years

Since I started covering CRDF, it has become more and more apparent that they are not that interested in announcing updates with no real meat. All the news we've seen (on the clinical front) has been pretty important data readouts with substantially more patients, or they have been presentations in diseases that were probably not on your radar.

Therefore, this promised phase 2 update coming in Q1 2026 (which continues to be affirmed as of their most recent corporate presentation) should be an important one. In particular, I'm hopeful to get a more thorough read on the durability of responses and progression-free survival.

Risk - The potential for delays

We've seen timelines drift considerably for CRDF's data readouts over the past few years, and that's made investors both nervous and excited, particularly given the potential for delays to mean better outcomes for patients. I believe this is part of the reason we saw the hype bubble burst pretty violently last July, as CRDF stock fell by around 50% and has since not recovered to those highs, despite the data readout from CRDF-004 being relatively good, all things considered.

If the data readout timeline drifts outside of Q1 2026 for some reason (and I have not seen a reason for that to be the case), I would expect substantial volatility, making it difficult to dial in a decent entry point for CRDF stock.

Risk - Cash not an imminent crisis, but it's not solid, either

CRDF is able to maintain a pretty healthy cash runway, as far as developmental biotechs go, through the careful curation of their research programs. As a result, they have surprisingly low operating expenses considering how far along they are.

Still, if they're to make any further progress on the onvansertib story, the research will get a lot more expensive. Running a large, randomized confirmatory trial is not a trivial endeavor, and CRDF does not have the kind of deep pockets companies need to get these off the ground. I would still expect a large fundraising effort in the relatively near future.

Bottom-Line Summary

CRDF is poised to release some important data in colorectal cancer within the next couple of months. This is exciting news, given what we've seen so far, and there are many questions that remain outstanding for the company that I'm hoping this update can resolve. Are the responses we've seen holding up over time? Do we have a clear direction for the phase 3 trial yet? Are patients doing better overall with onvansertib?

All of this points to the next readout being potentially momentous for CRDF. For the near term, it's the most likely inflection point, and although I suspect that a positive readout would have some kind of fundraising effort tied to it (attempting to do an equity raise from a position of strength), this data update could be the one that defines the direction for the phase 3 trial, which is what the company really needs to begin to get onvansertib potentially into the market.

As such, we find ourselves in a rather precarious position. If this readout affirms the duration of response and continues to support the response rates we saw in July, then I suspect the market will receive the news very enthusiastically. But there is substantial risk that this data readout will also be underwhelming, possibly creating further delays for the beginning of the phase 3 trial. For now, the market is pricing in some level of success relative to their finances (ie, comparing the $60 million in liquidity against their $200 million market cap), but as I've discussed before, the size of the market for first-line colorectal cancer treatment (something like $13 billion in 2023) speaks to the strong potential here. I affirm a "Buy" sentiment at these levels, but we should be very cautious about the risks here, as well, if the data readout coming soon.

data.title: Lumen: Major Questions For 2026
data.description:
Of the stocks I have covered on this site, one of the biggest rallies we saw last year came from Lumen Technologies (LUMN). The communication services company went from a low of $3.01 to nearly $12 at its peak, before falling back a bit, as investors welcomed major turnaround progress. As we now look at 2026, there are three major questions I have that will shape whether the stock can get back into the double digits.

Previous coverage of the name

My most recent article on Lumen came back in August after the company reported its second quarter results, at which point I raised my rating to a buy. While revenues were still declining and set to go further lower, I liked the progress being made on cost and debt reductions, and thought the valuation was quite reasonable. Even though shares have lost more than a third from their 52-week high, they still are up 111% since that August article (through Friday's close), compared to a 10% gain for the S&P 500.

The major 2026 questions

Perhaps the biggest item for this year is the company's revenue situation. As the chart below shows, revenues in 2026 are currently estimated to be less than half of the 2028 peak. This is due to a combination of declines in the business as well as numerous divestitures and asset sales. Lumen is in the process of selling its consumer fiber business to AT&T (T), which will mean another leg lower for the top line. The blue bars below represent current street estimates, and we should have full 2025 numbers in a month or so.

The timing of the fiber sale could impact things a bit in 2027, especially if the sale isn't finalized for another couple of months. However, management needs to detail whether this will be the last major asset sale for the time being. In the last few years, every time I thought revenues were about to start leveling off, the company announced another major divestiture that sent the top line even lower. Currently, wall street analysts don't see yearly revenue growth coming until 2030, but investors are hoping it's much sooner than that.

At the Q4 earnings report, we'll get the company's initial 2026 guidance. I'm most curious to see what the cash flow picture will look like. The fiber sale was expected to reduce capital expenditures a bit, but what other impacts will it have on the financials? If Lumen can have another year where it generates a billion dollars or more of free cash flow, it could really help the overall debt picture, outside of the asset sale proceeds being used to reduce borrowings. The company recently completed a major refinancing to push some debts due in 2029 through 2031 out to 2036, although the transaction will result in $66 million in added interest costs a year.

Lumen finished Q3 with about $2.4 billion in total cash and over $17.6 billion in total debt. Management was expecting a $4.8 billion reduction in total debt from the AT&T sale. Another good year or two or free cash flow could potentially get total borrowings down towards the $10 billion area. It does help a bit that Fed rate cuts have reduced the interest costs on the company's variable rate borrowings. The debt reduction expected this year could end up saving a few hundred million on annual interest expenses.

If management truly believes the worst days are behind us, I'm curious to see whether the company could restart a dividend plan. When I first covered Lumen several years ago, it was an income investor favorite, with an annual yield at times in the low double digits. The dividend was cut and eliminated as cash burn took a toll on the balance sheet, but perhaps it is time to come back. Even just a penny a quarter could be a major signal to investors that good things are ahead, and it would only cost about $41 million a year.

A look at the valuation picture

As of Friday's close, Lumen had a valuation of $8.11 billion. Since the company is not profitable at the moment, the best way to look at things is price to sales. The stock currently fetches 0.70 times its expected revenues for this year, whereas a name like AT&T goes for 1.34 times and Verizon (VZ) goes for 1.21 times. With Lumen having declining revenues and a bit shakier financial situation, it does seem that some level of a discount is appropriate.

At the moment, 8 of the 11 street analysts covering Lumen have hold ratings on the stock, with 2 having some kind of buy rating and 1 sell. The average price target is $7.78, which implies about 1.6% downside from last week's finish. That average is up substantially from the less than $5 it was at when I last covered the name, but back in late 2022 analysts thought the name was worth low double digits.

Biggest current risks

When it comes to current risks, the biggest one now is that management continues to divest some of the company's legacy businesses. If this occurs, total revenues could easily drop under the $10 billion annual mark, and we could see another hit to annual free cash flow. Lumen is at the point where the investments made over the past year or two are hopefully about to start paying off. Another asset sale that pushes revenue estimates meaningfully lower again might cause those investors who bought into the turnaround to take their profits and move on.

The second major risk is that Lumen cannot reduce its capital expenditures as much as hoped. This is a very capital intensive industry, and if the company has to spend too much to expand its current fiber network, cash flows for the rest of the decade will be pressured. For now, there aren't any major debt maturities in the near term, but as we get closer to 2030 if the cash flow situation worsens then concerns over the balance sheet will rise again.

Final thoughts and recommendation

After a strong rally in 2025 came to a halt, it's time for Lumen management to get this train back on the tracks. Things certainly look a lot better now than they did a couple of years ago, but it would be nice to see some revenue growth come at some point. The balance sheet is getting there, and the AT&T deal will certainly help reduce the debt pile. 2026 will be another transition year thanks to the fiber sale, but it could lay the foundation for a solid future.

With a reasonable valuation and hopefully brighter days ahead, I am going to leave my buy rating on the stock for now. I would like to see a little more evidence that the revenue declines are ending sooner rather than later, and perhaps a dividend restart would boost overall investor sentiment. Hopefully management will provide decent initial guidance for 2026, and perhaps be able to build on that as the year progresses.

data.title: Blue Owl Capital: Betting On AI While Navigating A Legal Minefield
data.description:
Investment thesis

Blue Owl Capital Inc. (OWL) is one of the largest players in the asset management segment, focusing today on the development of AI through the leasing of data centers to major hyperscalers. But this is one side of the business that's facing new risks and threats. It's not just about the cycle of falling rates or the rise in problem loans, it's also about potential lawsuits brought by shareholders through law firms. I recommend a "Hold" rating for OWL shares. It can be seen as a positive short-term outlook, since technical analysis of the 1W chart allows you to buy the asset in the zone of interest with the aim of growing to $20-21. Nevertheless, it is prudent to consider this investment speculative, as in the longer term, OWL may experience a new bearish trend due to the escalation of existing issues. All of the fundamental factors that became obstacles in 2025 remain relevant today. It's just that investors tend to forget about them pretty quickly.

Blue Owl Capital's strategy and growth drivers

Blue Owl Capital is one of the leading players in the alternative asset management segment. Its current asset portfolio is worth $296 billion, specializing in providing private capital to companies and financing investment projects for institutional and private investors. The main strategic transformation of OWL took place in 2021 with the merger of Owl Rock Capital and Dyal Capital, following which the company's three main business areas were formed.

  • Platform Credit (AUM: $152.1 billion) is involved in direct lending, where OWL provides loans to medium-sized businesses. It is a BDC segment in which the company merged its organizations (OBDC and OTF) in 2025.
  • GP Strategic Capital Platform (AUM: $68.8 billion) is associated with the acquisition of minority stakes in other private investment companies. OWL acts as an investor for investors, receiving a share of the commission for the work of large funds.
  • Real Assets Platform (AUM: $74.7 billion) is associated with transactions in the real estate and digital infrastructure leaseback market. Within this area, OWL acquired IPI Partners in 2025, which allowed it to become one of the largest owners of data centers and infrastructure services for cloud computing and AI operators.

In essence, the OWL earns money from commissions rather than from the sale of assets. That sets the company apart from traditional PE funds, where profits come from the sale of assets. OWL's entire revenue (USD 2.549 billion according to data for Q3 2025) comes from management fees, the performance of which is stable due to long-term cooperation with client partners. As a result, the OWL stock is less sensitive to financial and economic cycles but more sensitive to the pace of new capital raising. However, these commission revenues are only increasing, at an average annual growth rate of +32%.

There are three main points I would like to highlight as catalysts for OWL's potential growth in 2026. Firstly, its specialization in AI supercycle infrastructure, allowing the company to participate in the profit sharing of the new technology boom. Its Real Assets platform is one of the largest landlords for companies such as Google, Amazon, and Microsoft. On November 11, 2025, the OWL increased its investment in OpenAI's Stargate AI project by $3 billion, enabling the construction of a data center in New Mexico. Previously, OWL agreed to purchase IPI Partners' digital infrastructure for $1 billion on October 7, 2025, which allows Blue Owl to expand its strategy in this segment. This transaction confirms that focusing on AI infrastructure is one of the key objectives of the company's management.

Secondly, the OWL has entered a new insurance market. Following the acquisition of Kuvare Asset Management, their Blue Owl Insurance Solutions division can now integrate insurance companies' capital into credit strategies. It increases the OWL's commission from providing private lending, with mandatory insurance in their subsidiary being one of the conditions for granting loans to customers. Thirdly, it is the fact that OWL has permanent capital that is not going anywhere. Its share, according to data for Q3 2025, is 72.2% ($213 billion) of all the company's assets. The value of perpetual capital lies in the fact that it will not be taken away by investors, making the current portfolio of assets and loans reliable in a period of high market volatility.

Economic environment

Since OWL operates in specific niches of alternative asset management, analysis of its competitive environment should be divided into two groups of players: general giants and highly specialized companies (focusing on each of the three business areas). Credit platform is characterized by the most intense competition, since here the battle for lending is fought with a number of private companies and their BDCs. Ares Management (ARES), a pioneer in private lending with a huge platform, for example, is considered the main competitor. Recently, I conducted a direct comparison of two BDC players, ARCC and OBDC, and the results of the total return over the past year show that ARCC is a more superior player in large deals in the BDC market than Blue Owl. My recommendation for ARCC is therefore "Buy," whereas for OBDC it is "Hold."

Another major competitor for Blue Owl is Blackstone's BXSL fund, whose operating principles are similar to those of OBDС. Apollo Global Management (APO), a leader in the income-generating lending segment, and Golub Capital also play an important competitive role. These giant asset management funds are all competing for the same clients as Blue Owl. Given the current cycle of falling interest rates, conditions for competition for the best returns are deteriorating.

For a long time, GP Strategic Capital was OWL's "crown jewel" as it was the sole leader and, in essence, a monopolist. By 2026, however, the competition had grown significantly. Firstly, the segment saw the arrival of Blackstone with its subsidiary Blackstone Strategic Capital Group, actively working to purchase minority stakes in management companies. Also, the subsidiary of Goldman Sachs, Petershill Partners, has been pursuing an active policy, focusing exclusively on GP Stakes. There is also a new player, Hunter Point Capital. Although smaller in scale, it pursues a fairly aggressive policy, allowing it to capture an increasing share of the market in this highly specialized niche.

Real Assets is characterized by OWL becoming the most serious player in the field of sale-leasebacks and data centers. Here, there is also competition, particularly from Blackstone, who, in my opinion, is very similar to Blue Owl in this segment. This is generally the most dangerous competitor for OWL in all three business areas. Just like Blue Owl, they're building a huge digital infrastructure portfolio, making them an important landlord for hyperscalers. Like, in 2025, BX bought QTS for $10 billion and put another $25 billion into developing data centers through BREIT and BPP. There is also competition from Realty Income and W. P. Carey, which are active in the pure rental and commercial real estate sectors. In the data center rental segment, these companies do not pose a threat.

Reasons for the decline in OWL shares in 2025

The downward trend poses a serious risk to OWL shares. Between January 2025 and November 2025, prices fell from a high of $26.73 to $13.25. This represents a maximum decline of 50.43%. According to Seeking Alpha, the price change over the last 12 months was a drop of 30.24%. Looking at the weekly time frame of the OWL chart, you can see that the price has been moving within a global downward trend channel all this time. Since December 2025, though, there have been some early signs of the price trying to break through the upper channel line. Also, it's hit a support zone ($13.25-$15.18) that I think should stop it from dropping any further. Technical factors are now forming on the higher OWL charts, which signal a potential reversal of the bearish trend to a bullish one. A resistance zone ($20.26 - $21.08) is the target for the recovery of the stock price.

The following events should be highlighted as fundamental factors that caused OWL shares to fall in 2025.

  • The blocking of redemptions, provoking a liquidity crisis. Back in November 2025, before merging with OBDC, the OWL blocked share redemptions in its OBDC II fund. The merger meant investors couldn't get their cash back at NAV. In the end, they would've lost 20% of the share discount.
  • Failure of the Oracle deal in 2025, in which the construction of a 1GW data center in Michigan fell through due to the lack of a final agreement between the companies. As a result, concerns arose about whether OWL would be able to monetize its purchase of IPI Partners.
  • The growth of financial problems according to the quarterly report for Q3 2025, where it is established that the share of non-accruals in the portfolio increased from 0.7% to 1.3%. This is a negligible indicator, yet it raises concerns about the quality of the loan portfolio. Moreover, the OWL's operating expenses increased by 19.5% compared to Q3 2024, and net profit fell by almost 50%.
  • The market has become skeptical about the further development of private lending. The era of “easy money” may be coming to an end due to high interest rates. These risks are increasing, especially given the recent news about borrower defaults.
  • Towards the end of 2025, several law firms initiated class action lawsuits against OWL, alleging that it had concealed information about pressure from investors who wanted to withdraw their money. Accusations of misleading statements about liquidity stability have also been made. These risks could lead to regulatory problems in the future, posing a "fatal" threat to the alternative asset management company.

OWL risks

I think the risks of investing in OWL shares are directly linked to threats that could affect how well it does. During the company's conference call after its Q3 2025 earnings report, the analysts and management pointed out a bunch of risks. The management stated that market trends do not pose risks related to Tricolor or First Brands, since the company has a stable loan portfolio and low sensitivity to Fed interest rate volatility. Analysts, on the other hand, have a different opinion, expressing concern about stock valuations, market sentiment, and the effectiveness of the digital infrastructure portfolio. This is because the company is betting heavily on this portfolio. And if the AI supercycle suddenly ends, company revenues will decline significantly. Therefore, the main risk for OWL is its bet on AI and the duration of this boom. Additionally, it is important to keep in mind the legal risks of lawsuits against the company by investors. In early 2026, news of new lawsuits emerged, growing in number and scale, potentially leading to a global dispute over Blue Owl's reputation.

Conclusion

As a result, fundamental analysis of OWL suggests that it has neutral investment appeal. Key factors for Blue Owl's stock recovery will be the resolution of current business issues and the successful realization of potential from investments in AI infrastructure and data centers. The technical analysis of OWL allows us to identify the current quotes as an area of interest for purchases. This transaction may therefore be possible, but only as a speculative one, with a target in the resistance zone of $20-21. Long-term, there are many challenges facing Blue Owl that could have a negative impact unless the previous issues that management and shareholders faced throughout 2025 are resolved. That is why my recommendation for OWL is "Hold," as it has the potential for both a downgrade and an upgrade.

data.title: Anheuser-Busch InBev: Waking Up After A Long-Term Hangover
data.description:
This year marks the 10th year for Anheuser-Busch InBev (BUD) since it merged with, or better said, acquired the operations of SABMiller. The promised benefits from that deal did not come through. This came amidst high expectations from the get-go, a tough integration trajectory, and a general movement away from alcoholic beverages, to which beers are no exception.

A lot of value has been destroyed with that deal, yet the passage of time and inflation heal many wounds, as low expectations warrant an update of the investment thesis here.

Expectations Crumble

Hard to believe, but shares of Anheuser-Busch traded around the $120 mark when the company acquired the operations from SABMiller a decade ago. Continued stagnation in the business and setbacks from the pandemic have meant that shares have largely traded around the $60 mark lately. This has not just resulted in 50% capital losses but marks a far greater underperformance versus the wider market and other asset classes.

Going back to the time of the deal announcement, the transaction was set to bolster revenues from $47 billion to $64 billion, with EBITDA seen up at $24 billion. That is ahead of synergies, all designed to create a global beer giant.

In between asset sales and adverse currency moves, revenues never hit the $64 billion mark. Between 2017 and today, revenues have come in anywhere between $47 billion in the pandemic year 2020 and nearly $60 billion in 2024, as it is evident that the business never delivered on these promises. This lack of growth is the issue, as the business continues to post very impressive operating profits and margins meanwhile. Lack of growth and leverage was the issue, as the business operated with a net debt load of about $100 billion following the deal.

This left the business struggling with debt, as the company resorted to divesting assets on the cheap, as well as entering into debt-reducing transactions. Frankly, this has been a key reason for the general malaise and underperformance of the stock, as big dividend cuts were not helpful either, together with the business facing tough operating conditions.

Despite all this, Anheuser-Busch remains the largest beer brewer in the world, including a dominant market share in North America, where it controls over a third of the beer market.

Gauging Expectations

Early in 2025, Anheuser-Busch reported pretty flattish 2024 sales at $59.8 billion, with organic sales up nearly 3%, but this growth in its turn was largely driven by pricing, with underlying volumes down a bit.

Adjusted EBITDA rose by 8% to nearly $21 billion as the business is very profitable, posting margins equal to 35% on that front, with EBIT margins reported around a quarter of sales. Adjusted earnings rose by forty-eight cents to $3.53 per share, driven by cost optimization and lower financing costs.

Net debt had come down to just over $60 billion, for a leverage ratio around 2.9 times, the first time in over a decade in which this number came in below 3 times. This opened the way for greater payouts to investors, including some initial buybacks as well as dividends, with a EUR 1.00 dividend in 2024 being hiked by 22%.

This was encouraging, together with the comment that the 2025 EBITDA was seen up in the middle of the 4-8% outlook, although after years of underinvestment, net capital investments were seen at $3.5-$4.0 billion.

2025: More Of The Same Struggles

Throughout the first three quarters of the year, the company has reported minimal revenue increases, albeit with 2-3% declines in volumes. This shows the continued trend of the business relying on pricing to drive revenue growth, with underlying volumes being down.

This, in combination with cost control, made it so that EBITDA is up nearly 6% through the first nine months of the year, albeit the third quarter was a bit softer on all these items. So far this year, earnings are up fourteen cents, making a $3.60-$3.70 per share number likely realistic, which works down to an 18-times adjusted earnings multiple, while leverage is seen around 3 times.

Upping Capital Allocation

Following tens of billions in deleveraging over the past decade, which has come at the detriment of dividends, share buybacks, and necessary capital investments made into the business, Anheuser is stepping up these allocations again after leverage fell below 3 times.

Early in December, Anheuser acquired an 85% stake in BeatBox, a producer known for fruit-forward Party Punch flavors, in a $490 million deal, intending to acquiring the entire equity in the years to come.

In the middle of December, Anheuser provided an update on the buyback program, with $400 million worth of shares being bought back between early November and mid-December, sufficient to reduce the share count by 0.3%.

At the start of this year, the company announced that it had reached a $3 billion deal to reacquire its 49.9% minority stake in its U.S.-based metal container plants from a consortium of parties affiliated with Apollo Global. This purchase includes about 7 locations, set to be accretive to earnings per share in year one.

Unfortunately, the degree of accretion was not quantified, with closing set to take place in the first quarter of 2026. This follows Anheuser-Busch actually selling this stake in 2020 for a similar amount, as it was bracing for the impact of the pandemic at that time.

And Now?

Frankly, lower share price development has made expectations fall quite a bit. In between a pretty flattish share count of 2 billion shares, a move from $120 (in 2016) to levels just half that, over $100 billion in equity was wiped out. Meanwhile, the balance sheet has been deleveraged by tens of billions as well.

By now, lofty valuations have reversed, as shares trade at a market multiple in the high teens, providing an earnings yield in the mid-5%s, and leverage is seen near 3 times. This looks pretty reasonable, in between reported growth, but Anheuser-Busch is not necessarily very cheap, as it, like its peers, battles with continuous losses. These are not so much inter-category competitive threats but are more related to consumers moving away from alcohol to healthier alternatives as the impact of alcohol is more understood and recognized.

In the grand scheme of things, it is evident that shares have been deleveraged and, frankly, trade at very reasonable valuations in my view. This comes as the industry has been seeing tougher times, just when the business was digesting its mega-deal. Currently, investors can look forward to growth and investing capital instead of deleveraging, yet in a difficult operating environment, I am cautious to become a contrarian just yet.

data.title: Aurora Innovation: 2026 Still Isn't The Year Of Autonomous Trucking
data.description:
Despite all of the promises of autonomous driving, Aurora Innovation, Inc. (AUR) hasn't offered the path to scale to attract investors to push the stock higher. The autonomous trucking firm has made some initial steps with launching trucking lanes, but the reality of limited near-term revenues has crushed the stock. My investment thesis remains Neutral on the stock, though Aurora Innovation has a potential double bottom set up around $4.

Still Walking In 2026

As with a lot of companies, Aurora Innovation has a crawl, walk, run approach. Due to the nature of autonomous trucking, the company was wise to start off very cautiously to avoid any disastrous crash.

Aurora Innovation ended the year with upwards of 10 autonomous trucks on the road and opened up a second trucking lane.
