In late October, Aurora Innovation expanded driverless trucking with the 600-mile lane from Fort Worth to El Paso and is currently working on extending this lane another 400 miles to Phoenix.

The business equation changes with the opening up a 1,000 mille lane from Phoenix to Ft. Worth. The autonomous truck can drive the lane direct without any overnight stops like with a human driver, dramatically increasing the time and cost for a delivery.

Aurora Innovation has long pointed out a financial assumption of a trucking company generating $1,510 in additional profit with the Aurora Driver. The autonomous truck equipment can complete eight trips per week versus only three for a human driver with higher costs leading to a 10% net margin on higher revenues versus only 3% with current human drivers.

The company has already completed over 100K driverless miles on the Houston to Dallas trucking lane, yet the plan for 2026 is to only end the year with several hundred driverless trucks on the road. Aurora Innovation has every reason to scale rather fast here, but the company still isn't making that move.

The second generation Aurora Driver comes out in Q2 with a 50% cost reduction. Aurora Innovation should have plans in place to start running, but the company is still in the crawl phase. Either the concept is proven out on the two existing lanes or not, but Aurora Innovation is still choosing to crawl.

The new Fabrinet hardware launching in 2027 will apparently allow the company to scale to tens of thousands of trucks while Kodiak AI (KDK) is already on their trail with 10 driverless trucks on the road. In addition, PlusAI (CCIX) plans to launch commercial services in 2027 and Enride (LEGT) already generates autonomous trucking revenues. Both of these companies plan to go public via SPAC deals this year as the competition heats up.

Minimal Revenues

Aurora Innovation only generated Q3 revenues of $1 million with a target of a similar $1 million for Q4. The consensus analyst estimates forecast only a bump to $33 million in 2026.

The company doesn't plan to fully implement the Driver as a Service (DaaS) model until 2027. Aurora Innovation plans to charge a fee for the service based on miles driven, generally at the cost of the human driver replaced by the service.

The big question is whether customers want to pay Aurora a fee per mile for the service, inclusive of remote assistance, on-site support and insurance. One might think large trucking companies would want to purchase the hardware and lower costs.

In essence, Aurora Innovation still has a lot of questions about the viability of the business model and this won't be answered until 2027 when the company shifts to the DaaS model. The company is just charging customers a flat fee now as the operator of the trucks.

The stock value has slipped to $9 billion, but Aurora Innovation no longer has the first mover advantage moat with plans for only a few hundred autonomous trucks on the road and a few trucking lanes open. Adding to the headwinds, the company keeps selling shares due to the large ongoing losses, which should encourage a quick shift to running the business already.

Aurora Innovation sold 80 million shares via the ATM to raise another $460 million. The company ended Q3 with a cash balance of $1.6 billion, but management forecast cash use of ~$180 million after $157 million in Q3.

The financials just don't add up for a $9 billion market cap and burning upwards of $340 million in cash in the 2H of the year and only targeting revenues of $33 million in 2026. Aurora Innovation needs to scale a lot quicker or the company is going to get into 2027 with a highly competitive market and just a few hundred driverless trucks in operation.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that Aurora Innovation might have some support for a bounce off $4, but the stock isn't appealing at this valuation. Investors need a far clearer gap with the competition to load up on Aurora Innovation here.
